item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the event of failure, redundant analog backups of this recording are to be stored at Archive Site ██.

This sounds like the analog backup storage will take place after failure occurs. I don't think sentences like these where the intended meaning Is so clear, but maybe the author would like to fix it.

Do you know what I love about the change in classification terminology here? I could immediately fill in the story: The old terminology was descriptive, and that could/did lead to propagation of memetic hazards. So now they use abstract names like October.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See this is why I read the posts in discussion before even considering voting. I finished this scip with a feeling of "this is just too damn goofy" and while not enough for a down vote I really didn't like it. Then I read the concept of this being a perception anomaly that results in the majority of people being unable to see the emus and my opinion of the article completely flipped! I love the idea of invisible emu friends for everyone but only a lucky few can experience them. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much belated, but: i feex. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoy this one. Just for the record, ratites are in infraclass Palaeognathae. The order mentioned is just ostriches; emus and kiwis are in two other ratite orders. I saw all the names and got distracted for like 10 minutes tracing out the taxonomy XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice but… well, same as always. I've seen better scips do far worse. I missed most of the references (the difference in cultural background may be the cause) so maybe that's what's keeping me from loving this as much as everyone else's. Having said that, take my upvote.
Hail to thee, memetic emu.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Having never lived in the UK I was unaware of such a person. In the context of this article his existance is a little twitchy…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ripping article, loved it to bits! Baffled by the complete lack of Rod Hull references/comments, though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want an emu. :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
a memetic perception disorder, or perhaps a memetic CURE to a perception disorder.

Or a little from column A, and a little from column B.

Do you think they really look like emus?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Lumancer, don't you dare regret this.
As Wishun said, the fact that their actions are synchronized such that all viewers witness a specific instance doing the same thing told me that this is not a memetic hallucenation disorder but rather a memetic perception disorder, or perhaps a memetic CURE to a perception disorder. My personal headcanon would be a multilayer reality (i.e. Samothrace or the Second Moon), if not for the fact that the emu companions of the noninfected cannot be seen by the infected, suggesting that the infection also has something to do with creating these, or at least each emu is so closely bonded to it's human that it cannot manifest unless it's human is willing to accept it, which most, of course, are not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was having His Dark Materials flash-backs before I even read this comment.

I saw them more as a product of perception, though. They were real as long as their surroundings all believed them to be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You had me from "persistent delusion".

Then you sealed the deal with "Harvey-Greens".

I adore. +1.

P.S. I remember that thread; been waiting for this for awhile. I love not being disappointed. Bravo!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To have the original author enjoy my spin is most gratifying.
You just made for a bright moment in a dull day, thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: oh my god

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am floored by that interpretation.

the birds are real, for some value of real

That much was intended, though I hadn't considered things on the scale you have. You may have just impacted my headcanon for this, and I wrote the damned thing…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have also decided that a twisted impression of the ever-present helpful companion with feathery wings is how the idea of gaurdian angels got started.

God damn. That explains SO MUCH!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Edit: I am very much in favor of creating, or at least hinting at, formalized classification systems. It adds a feeling of depth and a larger wrold beyond the snippets we see in articles. The mention that the system is being revised is a particularly effective bit of world-building while still allowing it to fit into any given head-canon.

DOH! I fail at commenting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like the Foundation is missing something here, and it makes for a good article.
They have it firmly in their heads that the birds are delusions, but everyone who can see them sees them consistently, and the original case left physical evidence. I'm choosing to believe that the birds are real, for some value of real, and the actual anomaly is that the majority of humans have been rendered incapable of perceiving their emu familiars. This extends to not noticing the physical evidence of on-going emu actions. Like when an emu helps carry the groceries, people just mentally gloss over the fact that they are seeing grocery packs floating in mid-air.

I have also decided that a twisted impression of the ever-present helpful companion with feathery wings is how the idea of gaurdian angels got started.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this.

My favorite detail is the reference to a shifting memetic classification system and the examples given, but I like the whole thing overall too. Nicely done, Lumancer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvote sealed at "accompanied at all times by a sapient adult bird."

I really, really like this, but I have to say the protocol names threw me off a little in the first couple paragraphs. I feel like less is more when it comes to that kind of thing, though not everyone does.

My favorite thing about this is that it's not apparently harmful in and of itself, and maybe would benefit humankind if left unchecked. The foundation of course must suppress it anyway because it's so damn weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this one, and the note about the GOC at the end clinched it for me. Something about it is almost creepy.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Correct. I have no idea what the draft in question was about, though I'm pretty sure it wasn't emus. All I know for sure is the one butchered word (as explained by Jukse) and the butchered phrase you mentioned, which is just too silly to use. I can't help but be reminded of the famous hilaribad digital translation example: "The spirit is willing but the flesh is weak" becoming "The meat is good but the vodka is lousy".

EDIT: the original article was about a memetic pocket knife before Google Translate decided to become my muse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much obliged, though that's going to be n awfully hard habit to break.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. Just a heads-up, I went ahead and changed all references to "amnesiacs" to "amnestics" in keeping with current site trends/theories.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lumancer linked the comment thread in the first post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy crap, this is the memetic emu. I seriously didn't believe somebody would make it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I believe it was inspired by it, but this is very different from the actual draft. I'm pretty sure "memetickemu" came from Google Translator messing up when Snowshoe ran the text through it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wasn't this from someone's sandbox, where a Czech draft randomly turned up? And there was a 'memetickemu' or something (with accent marks)? And 'it's so far out of control SCP Foundation'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Had me at "Type October memetic contagion" because I love that sort of stuff. I actually started wondering if the emus were real before I reached the end, and the hint of vindication I got was a great feeling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's even better is all the evidence from the village that the emus somehow had physical existence. And there's no way to test this, because the damn GOC killed everyone in the village.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I particularly like the quinceañera comparison. This is goofy and it works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not feeling it.

item.title: Re: Edit
item.content:encoded: Ah, of course. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Better than an emetic emu, I suppose…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ALL HAIL THE MEMETIC EMU

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, I can empathize bearing most of the stuff I put out. I can empathize, hypocritical, but I can empathize.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nothing to apologize for, this is extremely silly. That it's doing this well baffles me, I was expecting a lot more negativity. Thank you for the sanity check sir!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry Lumancer, the concept just doesn't work for me.

item.title: Re: Edit
item.content:encoded: In some stories about the GOC, their code name for reality benders is "type greens".

item.title: Re: Edit
item.content:encoded:
No problem.

I think the thing about this SCP that tickles my imagination the most is the fact that the meme was discovered in Peru and yet none of the flightless birds manifested is even from the New World, let alone the South American continent.

The Harvey reference was gold, though I don't understand why his last name is Green.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You had me at "Emu". +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is possibly the first SCP I would want in real life but still upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I chuckled my whole way through this.

But seriously, this is one of the better memetic scips I've seen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article made me happy. +1

item.title: Re: Edit
item.content:encoded: …huh. Odd convention that, since they're still frequently Latin, but there it is regardless. Thanks.

item.title: Edit
item.content:encoded:
Fixed two spelling errors.

"eg," needs periods as well as a comma.

Also, FYI, ranks of biological classification above genus are not italicized.

Also, +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Valid point. It was only in there as a callback to the prevalence of blind, deaf, or otherwise mutilated guards in older articles, which in retrospect is not a tradition I particularly want to poke at…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am okay with this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a pretty neat memetic SCP, and it uses the term correctly, which is always nice to see. The only line I would get rid of is this:

(or equivalent natural hearing loss)

Hearing loss isn't really clear-cut most of the time, and the Foundation is the type of organization that wouldn't leave any chance of infection if it can be easily prevented through protection. Even if someone does suffer from hearing loss, the Foundation would most likely make them wear the hearing protection anyway, as I see it. Not really all that important, but it just seems a bit out of place.

That typo was bugging the shit outta me.- Scantron

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Derp, thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On DATE

You may wanna fix that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. The codified meme terminology is a nice touch, too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I blame Snowshoe.
